text:
In this work the effect of different solvent systems was investigated for the recovery of antioxidants from black mulberry (Morus nigra), blackberry (Rubus ulmifolius) and strawberry (Fragaria x ananassa). By determining the levels of the total phenolic content, total anthocyanins and their respective antioxidant activity, measured through FRAP, DPPH• and ORAC assays, on different fruit extracts, effective extraction procedures were demonstrated. By applying the Principal Component Analysis (PCA) technique the effect of different solvent systems on the amounts of extracted antioxidants, as well as the pattern of the studied treatments were depicted in a 3 dimensional space.

The most effective solvent for the extraction of phenolic compounds from all berries was found to be 70% acetone/30% water (v/v) and the highest amount of anthocyanins were extracted using acidified methanol (methanol/water/acetic acid 70:29.5:0.5 v/v/v). Different optimised solvents were required to extract antioxidants from differing fruits; 50% ethanol for black mulberries, acidified 70% acetone for blackberries and 50% acetone for strawberries. Bioactive compound content was highest in black mulberries. PCA confirmed that the organic solvent-water mixtures generally gave better extraction than the pure organic solvent; and that hydration and polarity of the solvent affected the extraction of berry antioxidants. The results indicate that extraction of berry antioxidants is highly dependent on the chosen solvent combination and that different solvents are required to achieve optimal extraction of different phytochemicals.
explanation: Moderate likelihood the text was AI-generated. The passage is coherent and technically plausible, but its highly generic, formulaic phrasing resembles AI-generated summaries rather than a uniquely human-authored paragraph.
